CITY OF ORANGE                                                FPPC
                                                                Nov 12  11:29 AM '91

CITY ATTORNEY—(714)5382636    744-5580                         FAX (714)5382807
                                                                      538-7157

                              November 7, 1991


Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street, Suite 800
Sacramento, CA  95804-0807

Dear Mr. Hallabrin:

     This letter is written on behalf of the City of Orange (the
"City") and the Orange Redevelopment Agency (the "Agency") and
concerns the extent of the Agency to exercise
choices in the selection of an attorney or law firm to act as
bond counsel in connection with an upcoming bond issue of the
Agency. We request your informal assistance regarding the
selection of bond Counsel.

     Applying our facts to the Political Reform Act (the "Act"),
Government Code Section 87100 et seq., as interpreted by the
FPPC, we believe that in our situation conflict of interest
principles do not operate in such a fashion so as to preclude
the selection of any particular law firm to act as bond counsel
on behalf of the Agency. In light of specific facts and the
analysis provided below, we seek the Commission's concurrence
in this conclusion.

                            Statement of Facts

     The City of Orange has established the Orange Redevelopment
Agency in order to plan and implement various redevelopment
projects in the City. The Agency currently contracts with a
particular law firm (the "Firm") which is a full service law
firm, to serve as special counsel to the Agency. One of the
partners of the Firm has principal responsibility within the
Firm to render legal services to the Agency. That attorney
(the "Principal Special Counsel") is compensated for his
professional services only by the Firm.


ORANGE CIVIC CENTER  •    300 E. CHAPMAN AVE.    •    ORANGE, CA 92666-1591  •    P.O. BOX 449

Scott Hallabrin
Fair Political Practices Commission
November 7, 1991
Page 2


     Neither the Principal Special Counsel nor any other member
of the Firm attends every Agency meeting. Although the
Principal Special Counsel does a substantial amount of work on
various projects as requested by the Agency, the salaried
in-house City Attorney serves as the Agency General Counsel.
The City employs three assistant city attorneys who also are
available to serve the Agency. Further, the Agency contracts
with other law firms and attorneys for certain other legal
advice and representation in Agency proceedings.

     Customarily, the Agency has contracted with the Firm to
serve as Bond Counsel. The Principal Special Counsel is a
partner in the Firm; however, the Principal Special Counsel
owns less than a ten percent (10%) share of the firm. Any
decisions made pursuant to the existing Agency/Firm contract
for attorney services will not affect other Firm clients.

     At present, the Agency desires to hire bond counsel for an
upcoming bond issue. The decision to issue bonds has been made
without consultation with, the rendering of advice, or any
other form of participation by the Principal Special Counsel.
The Agency has issued a request for proposals ("RFP") for bond
counsel. The Agency has distributed the RFP to a
staff-prepared list of twelve (12) law firms which recognized
expertise in respect to financing matters, including the Firm.
The Agency staff compiled the distribution list without any
input, advice or participation by the Principal Special Counsel
or other members of the Firm. It is our understanding that the
Firm intends to answer the RFP and submit a proposal to act as
bond counsel for the Agency, either pursuant to the terms of an
existing contract or an amended or new contract.
Neither the Firm nor the Principal Special Counsel will
participate in or otherwise attend to influence the criteria,
the evaluation or the selection of the bond counsel.

     For the purposes of this discussion, the Agency assumes
arguendo that the Principal Special Counsel is a "consultant"
and thereby a "public official" under the Act.

                              Analysis

     With regard to the issue of whether the Agency may contract
with the Firm to serve as bond counsel given the existing
Agency/Firm contract for attorney services, we request your
advice in the form of informal assistance regarding two
specific questions:

Scott Hallabrin
Fair Political Practices Commission
November 7, 1991
Page 3


     1.     May the Agency contract with the Firm for bond
counsel services if the Principal Special Counsel does not
make, participate in the making or otherwise attempt to
influence the Agency's selection of the Firm?

     2.     May the Agency ask the Firm to serve as bond
counsel within the parameters of the existing Agency/Firm
contract for attorney services?

     This question contemplates that the Firm bond counsel
services do not constitute a new contract, but rather,
represent a further implementation of the role inherent in the
existing Agency/Firm contract for attorney services.

A.     Principal Special Counsel Participation and Influence
       in a Private Capacity

     May the Agency contract with the Firm for bond counsel
services if the Principal Special Counsel does not make,
participate in the making or otherwise attempt to influence the
Agency's selection of the Firm?

     We believe the applicability of the private capacity
exceptions inherent in the Political Reform Act and the fact
that the Principal Special Counsel does not make, participate
in the making or otherwise attempt to influence either the
decision to issue bonds or the bond counsel selection process
dictate that no conflict arises.  Accordingly, if selected by
the Agency, the Firm may serve as bond counsel within the
parameters of the Act's conflict of interest provisions.

     1.     The Private Capacity Exceptions

          Even if it were to be assumed that Principal
Special Counsel were to ask that the Firm be considered for
particular work on behalf of the Agency, no infirmity under the
Act would be presented.  The Principal Special Counsel may
participate or influence a governmental decision under the Act
despite a material financial interest if the Principal Special
Counsel acts solely in his "private capacity."  Regulations
18700(d) and 18700.1(b) define the "private capacity"
exceptions.  Regulation 18700(d) provides in pertinent part:

     (d)  Making or participating in the making
of a governmental decision shall not include:

                    . . .

Scott Hallabrin
Fair Political Practices Commission
November 7, 1991
Page 4


(2) Appearance by a public official as a
member of the general public before an agency in
the course of its prescribed governmental
function to represent himself or herself on
matters related to his or her personal interests;
or

(3) Actions by public officials, employees,
or employee representatives relating to their
compensation or the terms or conditions of their
employment or contract.

In addition Regulation 18700.1 provides in relevant part:

(b) An official is not attempting to use his
or her official position to influence a
governmental decision of an agency if the
official:

. . .

(3) Negotiates his or her compensation or
the terms and conditions of his or her employment
or contract.

The FPPC has interpreted these Regulations to
find that "the Act does not prohibit [a] firm's employees from
negotiating the firm's contract with the county, so long as
they do so while acting solely in their private capacity. The
contracting decisions on the part of the county must be made by
county officials." (See Workman Letter, No. I-87-078.)

Applying these principles, the FPPC concluded
that "it is appropriate under the Act for the county to
contract with a private firm to receive both consulting
services and supplies, so long as appropriate disinterested
county officials make those decisions." (Id.)

The FPPC reached a similar conclusion in Leidigh
Advice Letter, No. A-89-320. In Leidigh, the FPPC advised that
employee participation in the modification of their contract
constitutes participation in governmental decisions. (Id.)
However, the FPPC reiterated its findings in Workman, supra,
and concluded that "the Act does not prohibit [the] employees
from negotiating the contract with the county, so long as they
do so while acting solely in their private capacity. The
contracting decisions on the part of the county must be made by
county officials." (Id.)

Scott Hallabrin
Fair Political Practices Commission
November 7, 1991
Page 5


     By analogy, the Agency may contract with the Firm
for bond counsel services as long as disinterested Agency
officials make these decisions. In our situation, the Agency
and the Firm have an existing contract for attorney services
and the Agency desires to engage bond counsel for an upcoming
issue. The salaried Agency General Counsel and the Agency
staff have established the criteria for the selection of the
bond counsel, have created the RFP distribution list and will
implement the RFP process and make the ultimate selection of
bond counsel. The Firm and the Principal Special Counsel will
be involved only to the extent that they prepare and submit a
proposal pursuant to the terms of the RFP established by
Agency General Counsel and staff, actions clearly relating to
conditions and terms of possible employment. Similarly, any
required presentation of the Firm's proposal would qualify as a
private capacity act.

     The Firm and Principal Special Counsel proposal
preparation and presentation constitute "private capacity"
activities under the Act. Thus, provided the Principal Special
Counsel does not make, participate in the making nor influence
other governmental decisions regarding bond counsel selection,
disinterested Agency officials could make the decision to hire
the Firm as bond counsel without creating a conflict of
interest.

     2.  Special Counsel Neither Makes Nor Participates in
         a Governmental Decision

     Assuming that Principal Special Counsel has a
material financial interest in the Firm, the Act prohibits the
Principal Special Counsel from making or participating in a
governmental decision involving that material interest unless,
as described above, Principal Special Counsel acts in his
private capacity. Regulation 18700 sets forth criteria for
determining whether an official is making or participating in a
governmental decision. With regard to making a governmental
decision, it states in pertinent part:

     (b) A public official "makes a governmental
decision," except as provided in subsection (d)
of this section, when he or she, acting within
the authority of his or her office:

          (1) Votes on a matter;

Scott Hallabrin
Fair Political Practices Commission
November 7, 1991
Page 6


(2) Appoints a person;

(3) Obligates or commits his or her agency
to any course of action;

(4) Enters into any contractual agreement on
behalf of his or her agency;

(5) Determines not to act within the
meaning of sub-paragraphs (1), (2), (3) or (4),
unless such determination is made because of his
or her financial interest.

(Regulation 18700(b).)

Neither the Principal Special Counsel nor the
Firm wield any authority to vote, appoint, obligate or enter a
contract for the Agency regarding the decisions to secure bond
counsel, to establish selection criteria, or to implement the
RFP selection process. As the facts stipulate, the Principal
Special Counsel did not make the decision to issue bonds and
will not make decisions regarding proposal evaluations or the
selection of bond counsel. Therefore, we conclude that the
bond issue and the RFP process present no opportunity for
Principal Special Counsel to "make a governmental decision."

With respect to participation in a governmental
decision, Regulation 18700(c) provides that:

(c) A public official or designated
employee "participates in the making of a
governmental decision" when, acting within the
authority of his or her position, he or she:

(1) Negotiates, without significant
substantive review, with a governmental entity or
private person regarding the decision; or

(2) Advises or makes recommendations to the
decision-maker, either directly or without
significant intervening substantive review, by:

(A) Conducting research or making any
investigation which requires the exercise of
judgment on the part of the official or
designated employee and the purpose of which
is to influence the decision; or

Scott Hallabrin
Fair Political Practices Commission
November 7, 1991
Page 7


(B) Preparing or presenting any
report, analysis or opinion, orally or in
writing, which requires the exercise of
judgment on the part of the official or
designated employee and the purpose of which
is to influence the decision.

(Regulation 18700(c).)

The FPPC applied these participation regulations
in the Workman Letter, supra. Workman involved a consulting
contract between a computer company and Glenn County. The
County wanted to know whether the company could serve both as
consultant and supplier of computer products.

The FPPC reasoned that "it is appropriate under
the Act for the county to contract with a private firm to
receive both consulting services and supplies, so long as
appropriate disinterested county officials made those
decisions." (Id.) Further, "assuming that the firm's
employees are not involved in the preparation of the criteria
for the bids, a competitive bidding procedure will decrease any
likelihood of improper influence over the selection process."
(Id.) Accordingly, the FPPC concluded that a firm with a
material financial interest "may bid on other work and
materials, so long as its employees do not participate in any
way in the preparation of the criteria for the bid or in the
evaluation of the bid responses." (Id.)

Our facts stipulate that the Principal Special
Counsel has not taken and will not take any steps to
participate "within the authority of his . . . position" as
Agency Special Counsel to influence any Agency bond decisions.
Specifically, the Principal Special Counsel and the firm have
not negotiated, discussed or exercised any judgment regarding
the decisions to issue bonds, establish the RFP criteria,
create the RFP distribution list or select the bond counsel.
Rather the salaried Agency General Counsel, assistants and
Agency staff have made these important decisions.

Accordingly, our facts fit squarely within the
FPPC's Workman analysis. The Agency, without the Firm's or
Principal Special Counsel's advice or judgment decided to issue
bonds, established the RFP criteria and will process and
evaluate the proposals and select the bond counsel. These

Scott Hallabrin
Fair Political Practices Commission
November 7, 1991
Page 8


facts afford no opportunity for the Firm or Principal Special
Counsel to exercise their judgment and thereby participate in
the Agency decisions regarding the issuing of bonds and the
selection of bond counsel.

     In summary, as neither the Firm nor the Principal
Special Counsel make part participate in the Agency decisions to
hire bond counsel and implement the RFP, a conflict of interest
does not arise unless they use whatever official position they
may have to influence one of the Agency bond counsel decisions.

     3.  Using Official Position to Influence a
         Governmental Decision

     The Firm's potential material financial effect
also bars the Principal Special Counsel from using official
position to influence governmental decisions related to the
material effect.  Regulation 18700.1 defines when an official
is using his or her position to influence a governmental
decision.  It states in pertinent part:

          (a)  With regard to a governmental decision
     which is within or before an official's agency or
     an agency appointed by or subject to the
     budgetary control of his or her agency, the
     official is attempting to use his or her official
     position to influence the decision if, for the
     purpose of influencing the decision, the official
     contacts, or appears before, or otherwise
     attempts to influence, any member, officer,
     employee or consultant of the agency.  Attempts
     to influence include, but are not limited to,
     appearances or contacts by the official on behalf
     of a business entity, client, or customer.

(Regulation 18700.1(a) and McHugh Letter, supra.)

     McHugh involved a situation where a city
councilmember owned a business which performs engineering and
surveying services for builders and developers.  The FPPC
interpreted Regulation 18700.1(a) to mean that "the
councilmember may not meet with the city manager or with
members of the building, engineering or planning staff of the
city to discuss the processing of the development projects.
Any contact with city staff must be limited to necessary

Scott Hallabrin
Fair Political Practices Commission
November 7, 1991
Page 9


contact concerning the processing or evaluation of drawings or
submissions of an architectural, engineering or similar
nature." (McHugh Letter, supra.) "Necessary contact" is
limited to responding to questions from city staff. (Id.; see
West Advice Letter, No. A-88-413.)

Neither the Firm nor Principal Special Counsel
have discussed with any Agency officials whether bonds may be
or should be issued, whether bond counsel is necessary or how
the RFP criteria should be structured and evaluated. Further,
the Principal Special Counsel will not initiate any contact
with Agency officials or staff regarding these bond matters.
Our facts, do not suggest any Principal Special Counsel or Firm
influence on the Agency bond decisions.

In summary, if the Principal Special Counsel does
not contact any member, officer or employee of the Agency
regarding the terms, the process or the review of the RFP for
bond counsel, the Principal Special Counsel will not exercise
his official capacity to influence the Agency's governmental
decisions.

4. Conclusion

As the Firm's preparation and presentation of its
proposal, in response to the RFP, fit within the private
capacity exceptions and because the Principal Special Counsel
will not act in his official capacity to make, participate or
influence any Agency governmental decision regarding the RFP or
bond counsel selection, notwithstanding a material financial
interest by the Principal Special Counsel, the Agency may
contract with the Firm for bond counsel services pursuant to an
RFP.

Therefore, no conflict arises, even though
Principal Special Council may contact Agency officials on other
matters. To conclude that such contact constitute a
conflict is to prohibit contract attorneys (city attorneys,
special counsel, etc.) from submitting a new contract or an
extension or amendment of an existing one.

B. Implementing the Previously Made Governmental Decision
to Contract for Attorney Services

May the Agency ask the Firm to serve as bond counsel
within the parameters of the existing Agency/Firm arrangement
for attorney services?


[illegible]

Scott Hallabrin
Fair Political Practices Commission
November 7, 1991
Page 10


     This question contemplates that the Agency request for
attorney services as bond counsel does not involve a new
contract, but rather, the implementation of the previously made
governmental decision to enter the existing Agency/Firm
contract for attorney services. Where a consultant is involved
in various on-going matters and new matters arise, the question
presented is whether and under what circumstances the
consultant may take on the new matters.

     We believe that the Act, as interpreted by the FPPC,
enables the Agency to request the Firm to serve as bond counsel
because bond counsel services clearly involve the attorney
services inherent in the existing Agency/Firm contract; the
Agency selection of the Firm implements the previous
governmental decision to hire the Firm for attorney services;
and the Agency makes the determination that the Firm should
serve as bond counsel. Contract modifications, if any, to
accommodate bond counsel compensation constitute private
activity under the Act.

     1.  Bond Counsel Services Are Attorney Services
         Within the Scope of the Existing Agency/Firm
         Relationship

          Bond counsel services clearly involve the
attorney services inherent in the existing Agency/Firm
relationship. Further, the California Redevelopment Law
specifically contemplates the financing of redevelopment
activities including bond issuances. (See Health and Safety
Code Sections 33630 et seq. and 33640 et seq.) While bond
counsel renders opinions as to the consequences and validity,
the services performed by bond counsel are similar to those
preferred by other attorneys. Bond counsel typically performs
services in addition to rendering the bond opinion. Examples
include: assisting in planning the financing and structuring
the bond issue, preparing enabling legislation or
constitutional amendments; obtaining from governmental
authorities such approvals, rulings, permissions, and
exemptions as bond counsel determines necessary or appropriate
with respect to the issue; and assisting in presenting
information to bond rating organizations. (National
Association of Bond Lawyers, The Function and Professional
Responsibilities of Bond Counsel (1983) at p.7.)

Scott Hallabrin
Fair Political Practices Commission
November 7, 1991
Page 11


The appropriate length of bond counsel engagement
will depend upon the issuer's overall financial program or upon
the nature of a particular financing. The knowledge gained
through working with a bond counsel firm allows an issuer to
judge, independently, a firm's abilities and quality of work.
Representatives of the issuer will have had an opportunity to
judge such subjective factors as thoroughness, creativity,
integrity, ability to work with others, and resorts to
pressure. (National Association of Bond Lawyers, Selection and
Evaluation of Bond Counsel, 1988 at pp. 6,9.)

Providing a wide variety of legal services and an
on-going relationship are important bond counsel traits
supported by our facts. Specifically, the Agency and the Firm
previously entered a contract to have a partner in the Firm
serve as Agency Principal Special Counsel. The Firm (and, as a
member of the Firm, the Principal Special Counsel) provided a
full range of attorney services to the Agency on an "as
directed" basis. The Firm is a full service firm that has
customarily served as the Agency's bond counsel.

These factors evidence the nature of the
Agency/Firm relationship. Where the Agency has traditionally
viewed bond counsel attorney services fit within the scope of
the Firm's existing relationship with the Agency, the Agency
request of the Firm to serve as bond counsel merely implements
the previous Agency decision to employ the Firm to handle legal
matters.

2. Implementing Prior Governmental Decisions

The Agency selection of the Firm implements the
previous governmental decision to hire the Firm for attorney
services. Once the basic decision is made, other activities
should fall within the context of implementing decisions. (Moe
and Dean Letter, No. A-89-454.)

In Moe and Dean, the FPPC found the decisions to
form an assessment district, to give the power to levy fees and
taxes, and to issue bonds to be "basic decisions." In
addition, the FPPC held that the "implementing decisions"
included advising the authority as to the merits of forming an
assessment district under one or more procedures, obtaining
requisite notices and waivers and drafting the resolution to
levy, and procedural questions regarding the bond issue.

Scott Hallabrin
Fair Political Practices Commission
November 7, 1991
Page 12


     The FPPC's Moe and Dean analysis did not suggest
that consultant attorney advice as to matters inherent in the
existing scope of employment with the governmental entity gives
rise to a conflict of interest. Although the complete Moe and
Dean contract facts were not disclosed, it appears that the
assessment district formation, tax levy and bond issue
questions would certainly impact the amount of work received by
the law firm under its contract as the joint powers authority
consultant. Yet, the FPPC allowed counsel without a 10 percent
ownership interest, to participate in all of these decisions
and stated that the facts suggest that no counsel decisions
would have a financial effect on the firm. (Id.)

     Just as a law firm can be employed to render
service to a public agency client regarding one matter, so must
it have the ability to render legal services on a variety of
matters. A law firm which, pursuant to contract, serves as
City Attorney routinely renders advice as to claims filed
against the City. It would not seriously be questioned whether
that law firm could represent the City in litigation arising
from the rejection of a claim, or in negotiating a settlement
with a claimant. The circumstances that the rendering of
advice could lead to more work (and thereby additional
compensation) cannot itself disqualify a firm from handling
such additional work where the firm operates pursuant to an "as
directed" contract. A contrary rule would be infeasible and
impossible to apply.

     In our situation, the existing Agency/Firm
relationship reflects the basic decision. Certainly, the
Agency contemplated a wide variety of assignments in
contracting for Agency Principal Special Counsel. As our facts
specify, the Agency has the discretion to request the salaried
Agency General Counsel and deputies to perform these services,
to enter a contract with another firm or to request the Firm
(including the Principal Special Counsel) to perform these
services. The Principal Special Counsel, as a member of the
Firm, serves the Agency on an "as directed" basis and provides
attorney services solely at the Agency's request. Given this
relationship, the Agency may request that the Firm do work
which ultimately leads to new work for the Firm.

     For example, the Agency could request that the
Principal Special Counsel render advice regarding the
feasibility of creating a redevelopment area. If the Agency

Scott Hallabrin
Fair Political Practices Commission
November 7, 1991
Page 13


makes the determination to create the new redevelopment area,
the Agency, in its discretion could request that the Firm,
acting principally by the Principal Special Counsel, be its
legal adviser throughout the lengthy plan adoption process.  A
conclusion that the Agency could not both ask the Principal
Special Counsel for advice regarding the viability of a
possible redevelopment area and subsequently request the
Principal Special Counsel to oversee the plan adoption
proceedings, would severely undermined the utility of all
contracts for attorney services.

     By comparison, an environmental firm should be
able to advise a city client of the requirements for an
Environmental Impact Report and also, at the City's request,
perform some of these tasks.  Also, if a contract city attorney
is directed to answer confusing questions and in the course of
performing work discovers defects in the general plan, the
attorney may notify the City of the defect and, at the City's
request, correct the defective plan.  Further, a contract city
attorney could recommend the City seek FPPC advice regarding a
problem and at the City's direction prepare the request.

     These situations are distinguishable from
situations which could arise in which legal counsel offers
services different than those for which attorneys have
particular or unique qualifications.  The Firm is not trying to
sell insurance to the Agency or promote other kinds of
services; rather the Agency hired the Firm to provide attorney
services.  Bond counsel services are consistent with the
existing relationship and are consonant with services which
attorneys provide.

     We believe the Agency may select the Firm to
perform bond counsel services within the scope of the existing
Agency/Firm arrangement for attorney services.  In effect, the
consultant's actions merely implement a previously made
governmental decision, the decision to hire the consultant and
thus, do not create conflicts of interest.

     Therefore, we conclude that the Firm's ability to
accept the new work within the scope of the existing
Agency/Firm relationship for the provision of attorney services
is a fundamental and essential element of the contract.  AS
bond counsel services are consistent with the existing
arrangement for attorney services, the Firm may serve as bond

Scott Hallabrin
Fair Political Practices Commission
November 7, 1991
Page 14


counsel if the Agency makes the determination that the Firm
perform these services. This would be the case whether or not
an RFP process is utilized.

     3.  Government Makes Decision

          The Agency makes the determination that the Firm
should serve as bond counsel. The contracting decisions on the
part of the governmental entity must be made by governmental
officials. (See In re Maloney (1977) 3 FPPC Ops. 69, Workman
Advice Letter, supra, and Leidigh Advice Letter, supra.)

          The FPPC has found that "if, . . . the contractor
could determine the extent of contract work for his private
firm, there would be a conflict of interest requiring
disqualification." (In re Maloney, supra, at 71.) However,
the Commission concluded that "a person contracting with the
county to serve as county surveyor-engineer has no conflict of
interest when he performs engineering or surveying work for the
county so long as he has no authority to determine the extent
of the work he will perform." (Id. at 70.)

          In the Workman Advice Letter, supra, the FPPC
concluded that under the Act a county may contract with a
private firm to receive both consulting services and supplies
so long as the appropriate disinterested county officials make
those decisions. (See also, Leidigh Advice Letter, supra.)

          The Principal Special Counsel, as a member of the
Firm, serves the Agency on an "as directed" basis. The Agency,
not the Principal Special Counsel or the Firm decides whether
to ask the Principal Special Counsel to perform a task or to
have the Agency General Counsel, deputies or other contract
attorneys handle the matter. In addition, neither the Firm nor
the Principal Special Counsel have any authority to vote,
appoint, obligate or enter contracts for the Agency. Instead,
the Agency's requesting that the Firm offer to provide bond
counsel services.

          Accordingly, the Agency determines the extent of
work the Principal Special Counsel and the Firm will perform;
the Principal Special Counsel does not make that
determination. As outlined above, determinations of the Firm
or the Principal Special Counsel regarding research and
procedures to answer various Agency requests constitute

Scott Hallabrin
Fair Political Practices Commission
November 7, 1991
Page 15


implementing decisions and thus, do not enable the Principal
Special Counsel or the Firm to determine the amount of work
they receive from the Agency.

In light of the foregoing and consistent with the
existing as directed Agency/Firm contract for attorney
services, the Agency will make all decisions regarding the
utilization of the Principal Special Counsel and the Firm for
various projects within the terms of the existing contract.
As the Agency, and not the Principal Special Counsel or the Firm
will make these decisions, the Agency may request the Firm to
serve as bond counsel. The Firm's service as bond counsel does
not create a conflict of interest because any contract
modification triggers the Act's private capacity exceptions.

4.     Contract Modifications Only in Private Capacity

Contract modifications, if any, to accommodate
bond counsel compensation constitute private activity under the
Act.

The previously noted private capacity exceptions
would apply as any contract modification would specifically
involve the compensation or the terms upon which the Firm would
serve, among other things, as bond counsel. Regulations
18700(d)(3) and 18700.1(b)(3) specifically conclude that these
Firm or Principal Special Counsel actions would not be
considered making or participating in a governmental decision.
Further, the Principal Special Counsel could not be deemed to
be acting in his official capacity to influence a governmental
decision when negotiating the terms of compensation and
employment.

If the Firm serves as bond counsel, the parties
could continue the existing fee arrangements as earlier
established or specify different arrangements. If the fee
arrangements are altered, Regulations 18700(d)(3) and
18700.1(b)(3) render the negotiations regarding the terms and
conditions of employment private activities. This is merely
reflective of the notion that fees and the scope of services to
be provided pursuant to a contract may from time to time be
changed by the parties. Accordingly, the Firm would act in its
private capacity and could serve as bond counsel.

Scott Hallabrin
Fair Political Practices Commission
November 7, 1991
Page 16


5.  Conclusion

     Bond counsel services are attorney services
within the scope of the existing relationship. Firm service as
bond counsel merely implements the previous Agency governmental
decision to hire the Firm to serve as Agency Principal Special
Counsel. Further, it is the Agency that selects its bond
counsel; insofar as the Firm or the Principal Special Counsel
would participate in discussions regarding compensation, the
Firm and Principal Special Counsel would act only in their
private capacities in these discussions. The Agency may
request the Firm to serve as bond counsel without creating a
conflict of interest under the Act, whether or not an RFP
approval is utilized.

                                        Very truly yours,

                                        [signature]
                                        Ronald L. Thompson
                                        Executive Director
                                        Orange Redevelopment Agency


                                        [signature]
                                        Robert O. Franks
                                        General Counsel
                                        Orange Redevelopment Agency

embedding.first_500_words: CITY OF ORANGE FPPC Nov 12 11:29 AM '91 CITY ATTORNEY—(714)5382636 744-5580 FAX (714)5382807 538-7157 November 7, 1991 Scott Hallabrin Acting General Counsel Fair Political Practices Commission 428 J Street, Suite 800 Sacramento, CA 95804-0807 Dear Mr. Hallabrin: This letter is written on behalf of the City of Orange (the "City") and the Orange Redevelopment Agency (the "Agency") and concerns the extent of the Agency to exercise choices in the selection of an attorney or law firm to act as bond counsel in connection with an upcoming bond issue of the Agency. We request your informal assistance regarding the selection of bond Counsel. Applying our facts to the Political Reform Act (the "Act"), Government Code Section 87100 et seq., as interpreted by the FPPC, we believe that in our situation conflict of interest principles do not operate in such a fashion so as to preclude the selection of any particular law firm to act as bond counsel on behalf of the Agency. In light of specific facts and the analysis provided below, we seek the Commission's concurrence in this conclusion. Statement of Facts The City of Orange has established the Orange Redevelopment Agency in order to plan and implement various redevelopment projects in the City. The Agency currently contracts with a particular law firm (the "Firm") which is a full service law firm, to serve as special counsel to the Agency. One of the partners of the Firm has principal responsibility within the Firm to render legal services to the Agency. That attorney (the "Principal Special Counsel") is compensated for his professional services only by the Firm. ORANGE CIVIC CENTER • 300 E. CHAPMAN AVE. • ORANGE, CA 92666-1591 • P.O. BOX 449 Scott Hallabrin Fair Political Practices Commission November 7, 1991 Page 2 Neither the Principal Special Counsel nor any other member of the Firm attends every Agency meeting. Although the Principal Special Counsel does a substantial amount of work on various projects as requested by the Agency, the salaried in-house City Attorney serves as the Agency General Counsel. The City employs three assistant city attorneys who also are available to serve the Agency. Further, the Agency contracts with other law firms and attorneys for certain other legal advice and representation in Agency proceedings. Customarily, the Agency has contracted with the Firm to serve as Bond Counsel. The Principal Special Counsel is a partner in the Firm; however, the Principal Special Counsel owns less than a ten percent (10%) share of the firm. Any decisions made pursuant to the existing Agency/Firm contract for attorney services will not affect other Firm clients. At present, the Agency desires to hire bond counsel for an upcoming bond issue. The decision to issue bonds has been made without consultation with, the rendering of advice, or any other form of participation by the Principal Special Counsel. The Agency has issued a request for proposals ("RFP") for bond counsel. The Agency has distributed the RFP to a staff-prepared list of twelve (12) law firms which
